title: Memory Addressing: LDR and STR
summary: ARM uses Load (LDR) and Store (STR) instructions to transfer data between registers and memory. This lesson covers direct, indirect, and byte-level addressing.
content_html:
CPU-Memory Workflow

The CPU's ALU operates on registers — not directly on memory. The standard workflow is:

  1. Load value(s) from memory into registers (LDR)
  2. Operate on values in registers (ADD, SUB, etc.)
  3. Store result back to memory (STR)

LDR — Load Register

Copies a value from memory into a register:

LDR R0, 0x00FC ; load word at address 0xFC into R0 LDR R4, myLabel ; load word at address of label 'myLabel' into R4 LDR R1, [R2] ; indirect: load from address stored in R2

STR — Store Register

Copies a value from a register to memory:

STR R1, 120 ; store R1 to word at decimal address 120 STR R3, myLabel ; store R3 to address of label 'myLabel' STR R3, [R4] ; indirect: store R3 to address held in R4 STR R1, [R0, #4] ; base+offset: store R1 to address R0+4

Word Alignment

LDR and STR must use word-aligned addresses (divisible by 4). Example: LDR R3, 0x81 is INVALID (0x81 is not divisible by 4).

Byte Operations: LDRB and STRB

For byte-level access, use LDRB/STRB — these do NOT require word alignment:

LDRB R3, 0x81 ; load the single byte at address 0x81 into R3 (upper bits zeroed) STRB R1, 0x82 ; store lowest 8 bits of R1 to address 0x82

Indirect Addressing

Indirect addressing uses a register to hold the memory address — the foundation of pointers in C:

LDR R0, [R1] ; R0 = memory[R1] STR R3, [R4] ; memory[R4] = R3 LDR R0, [R1, #4] ; R0 = memory[R1 + 4] (base + immediate offset)

Little-Endian Memory

ARMlite uses little-endian byte ordering — the least significant byte is at the lowest memory address:

EndianByte orderMost significant byte
Big-endianMSB at lowest addressFirst in memory
Little-endian (ARM)LSB at lowest addressLast in memory

exercises.title: LDR function
exercises.options_json: ["Stores R0 to address 0x100", "Loads value from address 0x100 into R0", "Loads address 0x100 into R0", "Compares R0 with 0x100"]
exercises.answer: Loads value from address 0x100 into R0

exercises.title: STR function
exercises.options_json: ["Loads value at myLabel into R1", "Stores value of R1 to address of myLabel", "Moves label to R1", "Creates a new label"]
exercises.answer: Stores value of R1 to address of myLabel

exercises.title: Word alignment
exercises.prompt: Why must LDR/STR addresses be divisible by 4?
exercises.options_json: ["ARM CPUs only have 4 registers", "Words are 4 bytes wide and must be word-aligned", "Only 4 memory banks exist", "ARM addresses are 4 bits"]
exercises.answer: Words are 4 bytes wide and must be word-aligned

exercises.title: LDRB usage
exercises.prompt: LDRB is used instead of LDR when:
exercises.options_json: ["Loading from a word-aligned address", "Loading a single byte from a non-word-aligned address", "Loading a 64-bit value", "Loading from a register"]
exercises.answer: Loading a single byte from a non-word-aligned address

exercises.title: Indirect addressing
exercises.options_json: ["R0 = R1", "R0 = memory[R1]", "R1 = memory[R0]", "R0 = address of R1"]

exercises.title: Little-endian definition
exercises.prompt: In little-endian, the least significant byte is:
exercises.options_json: ["At the highest address", "At the lowest address", "In the middle", "Not stored"]

exercises.title: Invalid alignment
exercises.prompt: Which instruction is INVALID due to alignment?

exercises.title: Workflow order
exercises.prompt: In the ARM CPU-memory workflow, what comes after loading values into registers?
exercises.options_json: ["Store to memory", "Perform operations (ADD/SUB/etc)", "Load from memory again", "Branch to another address"]
